questions.question: Explain the fundamental difference between deleting a node from an array-based list versus a singly linked list. Include a discussion on time complexity for each operation.
keyPoints: Array deletion requires shifting all subsequent elements to fill the gap, resulting in an O(n) worst-case time complexity.
Linked list deletion involves updating the 'next' pointer of the previous node to bypass the target node, an O(1) operation if you have a direct reference, but O(n) to find the node.
The key difference is the need for data shifting in arrays versus pointer manipulation in linked lists.

questions.question: Describe the step-by-step process to insert a new node with value '25' at the beginning of an empty linked list and then at the end of a non-empty linked list.
keyPoints: For an empty list: create the new node, set the head pointer to point to this new node, and ensure its 'next' pointer is null.
For the end of a non-empty list: traverse the list starting from the head until the current node's 'next' pointer is null (i.e., you've reached the last node).
Set the 'next' pointer of this last node to point to the new node, and ensure the new node's 'next' pointer is null.

questions.question: A common operation is to display all elements in a data structure. Compare and contrast the algorithms for traversing and printing all elements for both a contiguous array and a singly linked list. Discuss the steps, the role of indices versus pointers, and the overall time complexity of each approach.
keyPoints: Array traversal uses an index variable that increments from 0 to (size-1) to access each element directly via its position; this is O(n).
Linked list traversal uses a temporary pointer that starts at the head and repeatedly moves to the 'next' node until it becomes null; this is also O(n).

The key difference is the access method: array uses direct/indexed access, while a linked list uses sequential access via pointers. No shifting or insertion is required for the display operation itself in either structure.
